title: Services, Units, Boot State, and Logs · System Lens: Journal Query
summary: Diagnose a service that is enabled but unavailable by separating desired boot state, current process state, dependencies, configuration, and logs. Planned artifact: Add and verify the linux journal query increment in a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Query journal fields, units, boots, priorities, time ranges, cursors, and follow mode without losing causal context.
Use status, is-active, is-enabled, show, list-dependencies, and failed-unit evidence before changing service state.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Predict Journal Query
steps.instruction:
theory case — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 5. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Journal Query contract for the supplied data.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for query journal fields, units, boots, priorities, time ranges, cursors, and follow mode without losing causal context.

content.text: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 5. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Journal Query condition decided your prediction.

content.value: Linux Kernel Userspace API Guide — Controls stable userspace interfaces and the boundary between command-line tools and kernel behavior.

Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: journalctl always shows every application log regardless of configuration.

steps.title: Bound Journal Query
steps.instruction:
theory case — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 6. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

After tracing the worked code, choose the Journal Query application whose preconditions and limits still match the supplied case.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for query journal fields, units, boots, priorities, time ranges, cursors, and follow mode without losing causal context.

content.text: Query journal fields, units, boots, priorities, time ranges, cursors, and follow mode without losing causal context. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Capture identity, path, process, file, and service state; predict parsing and scope; run one safe simulated observation; interpret exit status; then change one bounded layer and verify rollback.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 5. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback. A defensible solution applies Journal Query, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet.

content.code:
systemctl show lab-worker.service -p ActiveState -p SubState -p Result
journalctl -u lab-worker.service -b --since '10 minutes ago'
# Restart only after evidence identifies a reversible cause.
content.caption: Runnable safe terminal evidence example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case

A teammate acts on this belief: “journalctl always shows every application log regardless of configuration.” Which repair addresses the cause?

steps.title: Defend the Journal Query model
steps.instruction: Write the theory evidence note for Journal Query: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 6. steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Query journal fields, units, boots, priorities, time ranges, cursors, and follow mode without losing causal context.
